claim_text: In contrast, hyperpolarized 13 C (HP13C) MRSI is an emerging technology offering a dramatically enhanced signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) over conventional 13 C MRSI methods by harnessing the spin hyperpolarization enabled by dynamic nuclear polarization of liquids26,27,28.

sub_claims.text: HP13C MRSI offers dramatically enhanced SNR over conventional 13C MRS methods via spin hyperpolarization enabled by dynamic nuclear polarization of liquids (dissolution DNP).

evidence.snippet: These polarizations correspond to an enhancement of 44,400 for 13 C and 23,500 for 15 N, respectively, compared with thermal equilibrium at 9.4 T and room temperature. The method can be used generally for signal enhancement and reduction of measurement time in liquid-state NMR and opens up for a variety of in vitro and in vivo applications of DNP-enhanced NMR.

The purpose of this study was to develop a hyperpolarization method that would enable close-to-unity polarization of organic molecules in a liquid solution. We describe a dissolved-phase, DNP-enhanced NMR (DNP-NMR) method, in which the nuclear spins of a sample are hyperpolarized in the solid state and subsequently brought into a liquid solution after rapid dissolution.
The SNR of the hyperpolarized spectrum was 4,592, and the SNR of the thermally polarized sample was 7.

Still, the SNR of the enhanced spectrum is 656 times higher (ratio of the measured SNR). Thus, signal averaging for 656² × 65 h (3,200 years) would have been required to reach the same SNR without enhancement.
The proposed DNP-NMR method opens up for a wide range of new NMR spectroscopy and MRI applications in vitro and in vivo because of the ≥ 10,000-fold enhancement of the nuclear polarization.

sub_claims.paper_value: ≥10,000-fold polarization enhancement (44,400× for 13C at 9.4 T vs thermal equilibrium; measured liquid-state SNR gain of 656×)
sub_claims.nuance: This is the seminal dissolution-DNP paper; it directly establishes the liquid-state 13C SNR gain via DNP that the manuscript paraphrases as "dramatically enhanced SNR… by harnessing… dynamic nuclear polarization of liquids."

remediation.suggested_edit: Optional: quote the widely-cited ≥ 10,000-fold figure from Ardenkjaer-Larsen 2003 to concretize "dramatically enhanced SNR" in §1.3.

phrasings_tried: dissolution DNP yields >10,000-fold liquid-state NMR signal enhancement for 13C
hyperpolarized 13C offers dramatically enhanced SNR over conventional 13C MRS via DNP of liquids
dynamic nuclear polarization of solids followed by rapid dissolution preserves nuclear polarization in solution
DNP-NMR enables new in vivo 13C imaging/spectroscopy applications by overcoming sensitivity limits

section_checklist.section: Liquid-state polarization (SNR comparison vs thermal equilibrium)

attestation.indirect_attribution_check: This IS the primary source for dissolution DNP. The manuscript's co-citation of ardenkjaerlarsen2003 alongside review refs (hurd2012, kurhanewicz2011) correctly credits the primary.
attestation.out_of_context_check: The manuscript uses Ardenkjaer-Larsen 2003 to ground the DNP-based SNR enhancement claim. This is precisely the paper's central finding; context matches.

sibling_verdicts.excerpt: Review of HP13C metabolic imaging via dissolution DNP; co-cite consistent with the SNR-gain framing.
Review quantifies ~10,000-fold SNR gain via DNP of liquids; cites Ardenkjaer-Larsen 2003 as the foundational dissolution-DNP paper.
